title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story / Te Whakairo i te Kōrero o te Awa
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. This stream holds deep cultural significance for Ngāti Kahungunu as a source of sustenance, a boundary marker between hapū, and a place where ancestors gathered resources for dyeing and carving. Students will connect with local pūrākau (stories) about the taniwha 'Mangakōtukutuku' who protects these waters.
learningObjectives: Create a collaborative whakairo panel that visually interprets at least one pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, demonstrating understanding of traditional Māori carving conventions
Analyze and explain how at least three specific design elements (e.g., kōruru, rauponga, unaunahi) in their artwork communicate cultural narratives and environmental values
Present their artistic process and finished work to peers using appropriate te reo Māori art terminology, explaining the cultural significance of their chosen narrative
Reflect in writing on how their artwork embodies the concept of kaitiakitanga and their personal connection to the local awa

activities.title: Haerenga ki te Awa: Journey to the Stream
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua who share pūrākau (traditional stories) of the stream and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students document their observations through sketching, photography, and audio recording, focusing on the natural forms, patterns, and textures that could inspire carving designs.

activities.title: Whakairo Wānanga: Carving Workshop
activities.description: Local master carver from Ngāti Kahungunu leads a practical workshop on traditional whakairo techniques. Students learn basic carving skills using soapstone or soft wood, practicing traditional patterns (rauponga, unaunahi) while understanding their symbolic meanings and connections to natural forms observed at the awa.

activities.title: Hōtaka Toi: Design Development
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop design concepts for a collaborative whakairo panel that tells a story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They must incorporate at least three traditional patterns learned in the workshop and justify their design choices in relation to the cultural narratives and environmental features of the awa.

activities.title: Mahi Tahi: Collaborative Carving
activities.description: Groups transfer their designs to larger carving mediums (wood or composite panels) and work collaboratively to complete their whakairo panels. Emphasis is placed on technical skill development, cultural accuracy, and teamwork throughout the carving process.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga: Exhibition and Reflection
activities.description: Students present their completed whakairo panels in a class exhibition, explaining the cultural narrative, design elements, and connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They participate in a group reflection discussing how their artwork contributes to cultural preservation and environmental awareness.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Master carver from local iwi for whakairo technique instruction
'Te Ara Encyclopedia of New Zealand' online resource for traditional Māori patterns and meanings
Carving tools, soapstone, wood panels, and safety equipment for practical work

tasks: Completed whakairo panel demonstrating technical skill and cultural understanding
Written artist statement explaining cultural narrative and design choices
Oral presentation using te reo Māori art terminology
Reflective journal documenting creative process and cultural learning

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Investigate the characteristics, uses, and meanings of objects and images from past and present cultures
Developing Practical Knowledge: Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and principles for specific purposes
Communicating and Interpreting: Describe and respond to the ways in which objects and images communicate meaning
Developing Ideas: Develop and refine ideas in selected art-making forms in response to local cultural and environmental contexts
